description: Pilot baseline: puzzle_001 (5x5 easy) across the full 2x4 matrix - 4 toolsets (none/fine/medium/coarse) x meta {off,on}, n=1 per cell, Opus 4.7 1M, max_turns=35.
motivation: Establish whether tool granularity produces any visible cost/behaviour signal at all before investing in replication.
formal_results: All 8 cells solved. meta-off cost/calls/time: none $1.29/3/359s; fine $1.45/65/281s; medium $1.04/22/277s; coarse $0.56/24/151s. Coarse cheapest and ~2.5x fastest; fine ~3x the tool-calls of medium/coarse. Zero meta-tool calls (later found confounded).
results_description: On an easy puzzle every toolset solves, so there is no solve-rate signal; coarse is the efficiency winner and fine is call-heavy but not proportionally costly.
conclusions: Granularity shows a cost ordering on easy puzzles but no solve-rate effect - enough signal to justify a replication gate. Meta axis inert here (later withdrawn as confounded).
methodology: Full 2x4 matrix (4 toolsets x meta on/off), n=1 per cell, on puzzle_001 (5x5 easy). Model claude-opus-4-7[1m], max_turns=35. Single puzzle, single trial: pilot only, no statistical claims. Run via harness/run_matrix.py.
